behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by filename prefix
behaviors.justification: While sorting logic could be tested in isolation, the real failure mode involves the full file discovery + parsing + sorting pipeline. A unit test might miss edge cases (e.g., '10.sql' vs '2.sql' — numeric vs lexicographic), how directories are scanned, or how filenames are parsed. Integration with real filesystem and database catches these failures.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test migrations/ directory with SQL files using numeric-order edge cases (1.sql, 2.sql, 10.sql, 11.sql, 100.sql). Run the migration runner against a test database and verify via the _migrations table that migrations were applied in correct numeric order, not lexicographic order.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on subsequent run
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: A mocked database could pass without actually storing/retrieving migration metadata correctly—wrong column names, incorrect table queries, or transaction bugs would slip through. Real database is required to verify state persistence and correct query semantics across multiple runs of the runner.
behaviors.test_description: Run migration runner against a test database, apply a set of migrations. Run the runner again against the same database. Verify that the migration count in _migrations table has not increased, and verify via database transaction log or audit that no migration statements were re-executed.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: A mocked database would never enforce true transaction semantics or rollback. The real failure is when changes persist despite an error, or when the error is silent and subsequent migrations still run. Real database transaction support (COMMIT/ROLLBACK) is mandatory to verify atomicity.
behaviors.test_description: Create a migration with an intentional error (syntax error or constraint violation). Run runner against a test database with existing schema. Verify that no orphaned tables/schema changes from the failed migration remain, that _migrations does not record the failed migration, and that subsequent migrations in the batch are not attempted.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: A mocked database will not enforce locking, making concurrent tests unreliable. REAL-INFRA: The core failure mode (duplicate applications, lost updates) is handled by database-level locking semantics—real DB with concurrent connections is sufficient. System level (actual subprocesses) is more realistic but not strictly necessary if the database enforces exclusive locks. Note: Some teams prefer System level for this behavior to test process-level resource cleanup.
behaviors.test_description: Open two concurrent database connections (in same process or as real subprocesses) both running migration discovery and application logic against the same test database. Verify that database locking ensures each migration is applied exactly once, _migrations table shows exactly one record per migration, and both instances complete without deadlock or partial application.

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the _migrations table is created before any migration file is applied
behaviors.justification: Unit test verifying 'call CREATE TABLE first' could pass even if SQL syntax is wrong, schema is incomplete, or table doesn't actually get created. Real schema operations must be tested against actual database to catch SQL errors, missing columns, and ordering violations.
behaviors.test_description: Start with a completely fresh/dropped database. Run migration runner against it. Verify that _migrations table exists after run, has correct schema (migration_name, applied_at columns, etc.), and that migrations were recorded in the table, proving the table was created before any migration was applied.
